method: Public source review only. No Inside Voice consultation was called during this phase.

candidate_decisions.decision: Introduce the Container Runtime Interface and make kubelet runtime-neutral
candidate_decisions.original_problem: Early Kubernetes was coupled to Docker Engine and needed a way for kubelet to work with multiple container runtimes without recompiling or embedding runtime-specific code.
competing_proposals: Keep Docker/dockershim as the privileged built-in runtime path.
Use a declarative Pod-shaped runtime API that lets each runtime drive desired state.
Use an imperative container-level RPC interface while kubelet remains responsible for pod-level lifecycle behavior.
candidate_decisions.why_final_approach_won: The CRI blog records that a declarative Pod-shaped interface was rejected because runtimes would have to reimplement kubelet features such as crash-loop backoff and because the Pod spec was evolving rapidly. CRI therefore kept pod semantics in kubelet and exposed imperative container/runtime RPCs.
long_term_consequences: Docker-specific dockershim became a temporary bridge rather than the architecture.
Dockershim removal later forced migration work but eliminated a kubelet maintenance burden.
CRI-compatible runtimes such as containerd and CRI-O became the normal integration path.

candidate_decisions.decision: Make Kubernetes API-extensible through CustomResourceDefinitions rather than only built-in resource types
candidate_decisions.original_problem: Users needed custom automation objects that behaved like Kubernetes API resources without requiring changes to the core repository or a fully custom API server.
competing_proposals: Use ThirdPartyResource, the early beta mechanism.
Use CRDs for generic API-server-managed resources.
Use API server aggregation for specialized implementations with more flexibility.
candidate_decisions.why_final_approach_won: CRDs offered the lowest-friction path: the main API server serves and stores custom resources, while aggregation remains available for cases needing more implementation control. KEP-95 shows the GA push focused on schemas, pruning, defaulting, conversion, subresources, scale, and compatibility.
long_term_consequences: Kubernetes became an extension substrate for operators and platform APIs.
The project inherited long-term schema, versioning, conversion, and scale obligations for third-party APIs.
Some features remained post-GA work, creating continuing documentation and compatibility drift.

candidate_decisions.decision: Move apply semantics into the control plane through Server-Side Apply
candidate_decisions.original_problem: kubectl apply was a central workflow but the client-side implementation was bug-prone, annotation-based, and unable to reliably coordinate multiple actors changing the same object.
competing_proposals: Continue client-side apply using the last-applied annotation.
Patch with strategic merge or JSON merge semantics.
Move apply to the API server and track field ownership with managed fields.
candidate_decisions.why_final_approach_won: KEP-555 argued that too many components had to change for a coherent client-side fix, the API schema implications were not systematically modeled, and the control plane was the right place to provide field ownership and conflict detection.
long_term_consequences: The API server became responsible for field management and conflict detection.
ManagedFields became part of object metadata and audit/logging considerations.
Controller development gained a typed apply path, but rollout semantics remained delicate.

baseline_weaknesses: The baseline ranking may mistake a very important API semantics repair for a more system-defining architectural decision.
The baseline did not fully test whether Kubernetes' strongest recurring historical motif is extracting provider/runtime/storage integrations out of core behind stable interfaces.
The baseline did not examine CSI or in-tree storage plugin migration deeply.
